coredata.dc:title: “ON-OFF” novel fluorescent chemosensors based on nanoaggregates of triaryl imidazoles for superselective detection of nitro-explosive trinitrophenol in multiple solvent systems
coredata.dc:creator: Jigyasa,
Kaur Rajput, Jaspreet
coredata.dc:description: Novel fluorescent chemosensors with a triaryl-substituted imidazole (TAI) moiety were synthesized, which exhibited strong fluorescence. These fluorescent chemosensors allowed highly sensitive and selective detection of nitroaromatic explosive, trinitrophenol (TNP). The TAI were successfully transformed to fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONPs) in aqueous medium using re-precipitation method thus enabling fluorescent based detection of TNP in aqueous medium also. We hereby present simple and selective chemosensors that are highly sensitive towards TNP and not-sensitive towards interfering analytes. In light of Stern-Volmer plots, Benesi–Hildebrand plots and 1H NMR titrations; it was confirmed that there exist competent interactions between the chemosensors and TNP. A detailed study revealed that synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer, electrostatic interaction and inner filter effect resulted in fluorescence quenching of chemosensors on the addition of TNP. The compounds are practically applicable for sensing TNP in real environmental samples as they gave good sensing response towards TNP in pH range of 5–9. Fluorescent test strips were fabricated which allowed effective on-site visual detection of explosive compound (TNP). To the best of our knowledge, this chemosensor represents the first example of TNP detection in aqueous medium using FONPs of TAI.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemosensor
Fluorescent organic nanoparticles
Triaryl-substituted imidazoles

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of chemosensors (S1, S2 and S3)
Fluorescence quenching titration in organic media
Fluorescence quenching titration in aqueous media
Visual detection of TNP on fluorescent test strips under UV light
Determination of stoichiometry and binding constant of complex
FONPs synthesis and fluorescence study of TNP in 90% aqueous DMSO medium

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful to IIT Ropar for NMR; SAIF, Panjab University, Chandigarh for ESI–MS, TEM; SIC, IIT Indore for CV; IIC, IIT Roorkee for FLS; DST-FIST New Delhi , for financial assistance for acquiring the FTIR, UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The author (J.) is thankful to MHRD and NIT Jalandhar for providing the research fellowship. We are thankful to Miss Priya Arora and Mr Harminder Singh for providing various suggestions in manuscript writing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthetic route for the synthesis of compounds S1, S2, and S3.
Optical property of S3 in different solvents.
Changes in fluorescence spectra of (a) S1, (b) S2, and (c) S3 on the addition of different concentrations of TNP in DMSO.
Stern–Volmer plot for the quenching of the fluorescence of (a) S1, (b) S2, and (c) S3 by TNP.
Fluorescence quenching response of S1, S2 and S3 towards TNP.
Job’s plot of (a) S1, (b) S2 and (c) S3 with TNP according to continuous variation method.
A plot of log[(F 0 −F)/(F −F ∞)] against increasing concentration of log[TNP] for (a) S1, (b) S2 and (c) S3 showing 1:1 stoichiometry for the chemosensor: TNP complex.
Benesi–Hildebrand plot of chemosensors (a) S1, (b) S2 and (c) S3 in response to TNP.
TEM images of (a) S1, (b) S2 and (c) S3.
Quenching efficiency of S1, S2, and S3 towards TNP in water.
Quenching efficiency of S1, S2, and S3 towards TNP (100 μM) in real samples.
(a) Influence of varying pH on the fluorescence intensity of S1. (b) Quenching efficiency (in%) of S1at different pH upon addition of 50 μM of TNP.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of chemosensors towards various nitro analogues (200 μM) and TNP (100 μM).
Fluorescence lifetime decay profile of (a) S1, (b) S2, and (c) S3 in the absence and presence of TNP.
HOMO-LUMO energy levels of chemosensors and analytes.
Test strip images of the chemosensor (a) S1, (b) S2, and (c) S3 on addition of TNP at various concentrations (i) blank strip, (ii) 10−3 M, (iii) 10−4 M, (iv)10−6 M, (v) 10−8 M.
Absorption and emission values of S1, S2, and S3.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Fluorescence quantum yields were measured using lophine as standard.

head.ce:title: “ON-OFF” novel fluorescent chemosensors based on nanoaggregates of triaryl imidazoles for superselective detection of nitro-explosive trinitrophenol in multiple solvent systems

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr. B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, 144011, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel fluorescent chemosensors with a triaryl-substituted imidazole (TAI) moiety were synthesized, which exhibited strong fluorescence. These fluorescent chemosensors allowed highly sensitive and selective detection of nitroaromatic explosive, trinitrophenol (TNP). The TAI were successfully transformed to fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONPs) in aqueous medium using re-precipitation method thus enabling fluorescent based detection of TNP in aqueous medium also. We hereby present simple and selective chemosensors that are highly sensitive towards TNP and not-sensitive towards interfering analytes. In light of Stern-Volmer plots, Benesi–Hildebrand plots and 1H NMR titrations; it was confirmed that there exist competent interactions between the chemosensors and TNP. A detailed study revealed that synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer, electrostatic interaction and inner filter effect resulted in fluorescence quenching of chemosensors on the addition of TNP. The compounds are practically applicable for sensing TNP in real environmental samples as they gave good sensing response towards TNP in pH range of 5–9. Fluorescent test strips were fabricated which allowed effective on-site visual detection of explosive compound (TNP). To the best of our knowledge, this chemosensor represents the first example of TNP detection in aqueous medium using FONPs of TAI.

ce:section.ce:para: For every country, their homeland security is of top priority. Almost all the countries are under dark thundering clouds of terrorist’s threat. That’s the reason that recently great research interest has been attracted to the development of new and efficient methods for the detection of these obnoxious compounds [1,2]. In addition to it, the detection of explosive is also crucial for environmental cleaning and military issues [3]. The need of the hour is to design such sensors that could selectively identify these compounds in the variety of mediums. Among the various available techniques for their detection that includes, surface plasmon resonance spectroscopy [4,5], ion-mobility spectroscopy [6], electrochemical analysis [7–9], X-ray imaging, surface enhanced Raman scattering [10,11], trained canines, etc.; the fluorescent based sensors find a steadfast spot. The main requirements of an effective sensor of being conveniently applied to in-field and real-time detection of explosive compounds, [12] are very well accomplished by fluorescence-based sensors.

The conventional techniques mentioned above are not accessible because of the requirements for expensive and sophisticated instruments, complicated manipulations, time-consuming procedures, low selectivity and poor portability. As an alternative approach, fluorescent chemosensors have attracted the interests of researchers because of their easy portability, simplicity, low cost, rapid response time and high sensitivity [13]. The literature is rich with fluorescent chemosensors for explosive detection that includes: conjugated polymers [14], metal nanoclusters [15], carbon dots [16], metal organic frameworks [17,18], quantum dots [19,20], etc. Although they serve as effective fluorescent chemosensors but still a lot of issues are associated with them that includes: low binding affinity towards explosive compounds [21], interference from other nitroaromatic compounds [22] toxicity and instability [23] and lack of applicability in multiple solvent mediums (aqueous medium) [22].

Chemical explosives mainly consists of peroxides (Triacetone Triperoxide (TATP), Hexamethylene triperoxide diamine, etc.); nitramines (1,3,5-Trinitroperhydro-1,3,5-triazine (RDX), octahydro-1,3,5,7-tetranitro-1,3,5,7-tetrazocine (HMX), etc.); nitro-aromatics (2,4-Dinitrotoluene (DNT), Trinitrophenol (TNP), Trinitrotoluene (TNT), Nitrobenzene (NB), etc.) [24]. Peroxide explosives can be easily synthesized from inexpensive materials. These were used in conjunction with drug crimes, amateur chemist accidents and terrorism. TATP is extremely sensitive to impact, friction, and temperature changes. It is considered as one of the most sensitive explosives known [25]. Nitramines like RDX and HMX have been extensively used in plastic bonded explosives (PBXs) and propellants. They pose high detonation heat, detonation velocity and detonation pressure [26]. Nitro-aromatics are main components of military explosives and unexploded landmines worldwide [27]. Tritonal, an explosive material is comprised of 80% TNT and 20% Al powder [28]. TNT and TNP are classified as explosives with high speed of explosion. They detonate at speeds of the order of km/s and cause a shock wave [29]. TNP is considered as one of the superior explosive chemicals that possesses high detonation velocity and also low safety coefficient [30]. IEDs (Improvise Explosive Devices) containing TNT or TNP have been widely exploited by terrorist groups for blasts in several areas [31,32]. The explosive nature of TNP has been exploited for a long time in preparation of lethal weapons like bombs, grenades, etc. The undetected residues of TNP are still likely to be deposited in anionic form as photolysis product in soil of war affected areas [33]. It is known to cause massive damage to human eyes, respiratory system, skin [34], central nervous system, cardiovascular system, [16] etc. It has been regarded as serious environmental contaminant and carcinogen [35]. Detection of TNP is particularly very important because of its massive use in the manufacturing of explosives (somewhat more powerful than the well known explosive, TNT), as a strong dye in the textile industry and as an astringent for the medical purpose [36]. It is also used in the manufacturing of trichloro(nitro)methane (CCl3NO2) or chloropicrin, a strong insecticide which possesses anti-microbial and anti- fungal properties [35]. The nastiest property of TNP is its exceptionally good solubility in water, because of which its wide usage results in drinking or surface water pollution, leading to systematic poisoning of human beings [35]. That’s the reason that for social and environmental safety, effective detection of a trace amount of TNP, both in solution and vapor phase is quite important [21].

The highly emissive conjugated small molecules offer some impressive benefits like simple protocols for synthesis, easy purifications and better batch to batch reproducibility [37], etc that crown them as ideal fluorophore moiety while designing fluorescent chemosensors. One such highly emissive fluorophore moiety is aryl substituted imidazole.

Going through the literature of fluorescent chemosensor it has been observed that most of the fluorescent sensors detect explosive compounds only in non-aqueous medium and their sensing ability was a setback when applied to the aqueous medium [22,23]. The chemosensor’s activities only in organic medium sometimes restrict its utilization in real-life applications, like environmental and biological samples [38]. To address this critical issue, a fluorescent based chemosensor that could detect TNP in both organic and aqueous medium has been suggested. For real-life sample analysis, the hydrophobic chemosensors are innovatively converted into organic nanoparticles through re-precipitation method [39]. Nanomaterials display tunable sizes, good sensitivity, and short-response time that offer improved activity of chemosensors towards desired target [38]. The organic nanoparticles have drawn considerable attention and have been extensively used for detection of biomolecules [40], cations [38,41], anions [42,43], etc in biological and environmental samples.

Three novel highly emissive aryl substituted imidazole derivatives (S1, S2, and S3) were synthesized in our laboratory. We envisioned that by carefully designing of these molecules with imidazole moiety, would result in the generation of fluorescent sensor that could detect TNP. Imidazole based fluorescent moiety have been reported for explosive detection in literature such as Santosh Kumari et al. [22], Roy et al. [44], and Sandhu et al. [45]. But to the best of our knowledge triaryl imidazole (TAI) derivatives have not been well explored for explosive detection. The TAI derivatives were converted to FONPs through re-precipitation technique [38]. The re-precipitation is the simplest, synthetic approach [39,46] in which, the solution of organic compound in organic solvent is slowly injected into aqueous medium while ultra sonicating it. The difference in solubility of organic compound in two solvents (insoluble in water) results in its nucleation and aggregation in aqueous medium [47]. This solvent displacement experienced by organic compound results in generation of an aqueous suspension having highly dispersed FONPs. The treatment of ultra sonication further assisted in formation of highly dispersed FONPs. The so synthesized chemosensors were able to detect TNP selectively over other nitroaromatic compounds at an appreciably low detection limit. The synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer (PET) induced by formation of electrostatic interactions between chemosensors and TNP along with strong inner filter effect (IFE) were the predominant factors for enhanced sensitivity of chemosensors towards TNP.

Syringaldehyde of 98% purity was supplied by SDFCL, 5-Methylfufural (≥ 98%) from Merck, 4-Allyloxybenzaldehyde (95%) and Ammonium acetate (97%) from Avra Synthesis, 9-Anthracene-carboxaldehyde (99%) from Acros Organics, Benzil (99%) and Dimethylsulphoxide (99%) from Loba Chemie. Milli-Q water was used for fluorescence studies in an aqueous medium.

Fourier Transform IR (FTIR) spectra were recorded on Agilent Cary 600 in the range 400–4000 cm−1. The UV spectra were recorded on Agilent Technologies Cary series UV–vis spectrophotometer with a quartz cuvette (path length, 1 cm). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on Agilent Cary Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. The slit width for fluorescence experiment was at 5 nm (excitation) and 5 nm (emission) for all fluorescence studies. Nuclear Magnetic Resonance spectra (1H and 13C NMR) were recorded at 400 MHz with the aid of make Advance 400 spectrophotometer using tetramethylsilane as the internal standard and DMSO d6 as a solvent. The following abbreviations were used to describe peak splitting patterns: s = singlet, d = doublet, t = triplet, br= broad, and m = multiplet. ESI–MS spectra were recorded on a WATERS Q-TOF MICRO MASS ESI–MS Analyzer spectrometer. Melting points were determined by Gallenkamp apparatus and were uncorrected. The nanomorphology was studied using transmission electron microscope (TEM) of make Hitachi S7500 instrument. Time-resolved fluorescence studies were performed on Horiba Jobin Yvon Time-Resolved Fluorescence Spectrometer instrument using time-correlated single photon counting (TPSPC) technique. The cyclic voltammetry was performed using CH instruments (USA), CHI620D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of chemosensors (S1, S2 and S3)
ce:section.ce:para: The compounds (S1, S2, and S3) were synthesized by a multi component reaction [48] as shown in Scheme 1 . A mixture of benzil (1 mmol), aldehyde (4-Hydroxy-3,5-dimethoxybenzaldehyde (S1), 5-Methylfurfural (S2), 4-allyloxy benzaldehyde (S3)) (1 mmol) and ammonium acetate (4 mmol) was dissolved in EtOH (2 ml). The reaction mixture was refluxed and monitored by TLC. After the completion of the reaction, the solid crude product was isolated from the reaction mixture through filtration. The precipitates were then washed with distilled water. The pure product was then obtained through recrystallization of crude from EtOH. The formation of products was confirmed by FT-IR, 1H NMR, ESI–MS and 13C NMR (Supporting Information Fig.ES1-Fig.ES12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching titration in organic media
ce:section.ce:para: A 2 ml solution of compound (S1, S2 and S3) with a concentration of 2 μM in DMSO was taken in a quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length. To it, an aliquot of analyte solution in DMSO was added. The λex was 318 nm, 310 nm and 307.96 nm for S1, S2, and S3 respectively.

Chemosensors were found to be insoluble in water thus sensing study on the compounds were performed in a mixed solvent system of DMSO: water (1:9) [45,49] at a concentration of 20 μM. The organic compounds were firstly dissolved in DMSO. Then 1 ml of this solution was injected into water while ultrasonicating the samples. The samples were ultrasonicated for 20 min. The FONPs thus obtained were present in solution containing 90% aqueous DMSO. The FONPs suspension (VDMSO:Vwater −1:9) was characterized through TEM analysis to confirm the formation of FONPs. The TEM images clearly indicated the formation of nanoparticles of organic compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching titration in aqueous media
ce:section.ce:para: The solution of chemosensor in VDMSO:Vwater (1:9) was titrated with an aqueous solution of TNP. The λex was same as that in non-aqueous medium.

1H NMR titrations were carried out by preparing two stock solutions in DMSO-d 6, one comprising of only chemosensor (S1, S2, S3 and S4) and the other containing an appropriate concentration of the TNP. Aliquots of the two solutions were mixed directly in NMR tubes and 1H NMR spectra were obtained.

The cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed using tetrabutylammonium fluoride as a supporting electrolyte, glassy carbon electrode as a working electrode, platinum as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. The scan rate was 100 mV/s. The CV curves were calibrated using ferrocene/ferrocenium (Fc/Fc+) redox couple as standard. The energy level of Fc/Fc+ was assumed at −4.8 eV to vacuum. The LUMO energy level was calculated using the equation: ELUMO = − (4.8-E1/2Fc,Fc + +Eredonset), where Eredonset implies onset reduction potential relative to Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The HOMO energy level was calculated using the equation: EHOMO = ELUMO-Eg,optical, where Eg,optical implying optical band gap was estimated from the absorption edge (Eg,optical (eV) = 1240/λonset) [50–53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of TNP on fluorescent test strips under UV light
ce:section.ce:para: TNP is known to contaminate the human body, clothing and other materials when used in the manufacturing of rocket fuel and fireworks [54]. In this context, the onsite detection of explosive is very essential [55]. To deal with this issue contact mode detection of explosive compounds is required. To promote convenient detection of TNP, we prepared fluorescent test strips. These were prepared by dip coating Whatman filter Paper (2 × 2 cm2) in DMSO solution of S1, S2, and S3, at a concentration of 10 μM followed by their drying in a vacuum oven. The dip coating was done with the aid of DELTADP-auto Dip coater at a down speed of 150 mm, Tank time 15 s, Delay Time 2 s and number of cycles 2. Later 10 μl of TNP solution with different concentrations (10−3 − 10−8 mol L−1) were spotted onto the test strips. After drying the strips under vacuum, the strips were observed under 365 nm UV light, and the images were obtained. Similarly, the so prepared test strips were also deployed for TNP detection in water.

For developing the desired chemosensor, we chose TAI as a fluorophore moiety. The limited utilization of TAI based chemosensors enthralled us to make its use in designing of efficient chemosensors for TNP detection. There are lot of reports of efficient chemosensors for TNP detection, but unfortunately they lag behind due to tedious multistep involved in their synthesis [1]. The so developed chemosensors in this work offer efficient alternative to those conventional chemosensors.

We utilized a simple one-pot protocol for the synthesis of substituted TAI, which were obtained in good yield. The three as synthesized chemosensors (S1, S2 and S3) were well characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, ESI–MS, FTIR, Fluorescence and UV spectrophotometer. The characteristic λabs and λemi of chemosensors, are shown in Table 1 . They exhibited impressively high stoke’s shift values when excited at their corresponding excitation wavelengths, thus showing that all the chemosensors have strong fluorescent property [56]. The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) was measured for S1, S2 and S3. The impressive fluorescent nature of S1 (Φ = 0.06), S2 (Φ = 0.07) and S3 (Φ = 0.23) [57,58], which is comparable to similar TNP chemosensors [59,60], encouraged us to investigate their potential use for the detection of TNP.

The compounds S1, S2, and S3 were soluble in many solvents like DMSO, THF, DCM, DMF, and chloroform, but were insoluble in hexane, ethanol, methanol and water. In order to screen the best solvent in which these compounds show maximum emission; S3 was chosen as a model compound. The compound S3 showed a prominent solvatochromic effect in term of fluorescence emission intensity as evident from Fig. 1 . Although it showed appreciable fluorescence emission in both THF and DMF but fluorescence emission intensity was maximum in case of DMSO. The λem remained almost comparable in all the solvents (THF = 389.06 nm, DMSO = 400.90 nm, DMF = 398.50 nm, DCM = 392.17 nm), except for CHCl3 (453.22 nm). A prominent red shift of Δλem ∼ 10 nm was observed for the emission in THF and DMSO. This red shift is also termed as positive solvatochromism which arises due to better stabilization of the molecule in the first excited state relative to the ground state, with an increase in solvent polarity [61]. This enhanced stabilization of first excited state can be attributed to H-bonding between the solvent and the imidazole moiety [62].

Considering the above results, DMSO was chosen as the best solvent for fluorescence study of S1, S2, and S3 [1]. The compounds S1, S2, and S3 showed impressive luminescence in dilute solutions of DMSO (Fig. ES13)

We also evaluate the optical stability of the compounds S1, S2 and S3. The solution of compounds were placed under a continuous 365 nm UV lamp illumination, then we calculated effect of irradiation time on their fluorescence intensity to ensure that their fluorescence do not degrade during continuous irradiation (Fig. ES14). The fluorescence intensities of the sensor did not change a lot within the limited irradiation time (60 min), which indicates favorable optical stability of the compounds [63].

Upon incremental addition of TNP to the solution of S1, S2, and S3 (in DMSO), the fluorescence intensity was gradually quenched up to the addition of 90 μM, 120 μM, and 130 μM; respectively, of TNP and then a plateau was attained (Fig. 2 ). It was accompanied by a change in fluorescence emission color of the solution from intense blue to dark (for S1, S2, and S3).

The quenching behavior of S1, S2, and S3 towards TNP were quantitatively analyzed with Stern- Volmer (S-V) Eq. (1):
(1) Io/I = Ksv [A] + 1
Here Io and I imply fluorescence intensity in absence and presence of TNP, respectively. [A] is the molar concentration of TNP and Ksv is the quenching constant in M−1 [56,64].

The S-V plots of S1, S2, and S3 were found to be linear at low concentration of TNP [36], which indicates that fluorescence quenching involves a static quenching mechanism at lower concentrations [66]. Herein at high concentration of TNP, the S-V plots deviate from linearity (Fig. 3 ). This deviation of the curve from linearity at high concentration is possible due to dynamic quenching. Interestingly, since fluorescence lifetime ratio (τ0/τ) remained unvaried (Fig. 14) over the complete concentration range, this deviation at high concentration would be due to occurrence of super amplified quenching effect [65].

For static quenching process, the quenching data can be further analyzed through the modified Stern–Volmer equation [67–69]:
(2)
F 0/F 0 − F = 1 + 1/K a [TNP]
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher (TNP), and [Q] is the molar concentration of quencher (TNP), Ka denotes the effective quenching constant, is analogues to the associative binding constant for the quencher-acceptor system [70]. According to the above equation (eq. 2), the quenching constant was found to be 5.69 × 104 M−1 (S1), 5.04 × 104 M−1 (S2), 2.22 × 104 M−1 (S3) (Fig. ES15). These high values of association constants indicate the super quenching ability of TNP towards S1, S2, and S3.

A fluorescence quenching efficiency plot between quenching efficiency (QE = 1- I/I0) and concentration of TNP was plotted. As shown in Fig. 4 the quenching equilibrium was attained between 70 and 80%, which is impressive. The quenching efficiency of S1 reached 95.69%, when concentration of TNP was 90 μM, whereas for S2 and S3 it was 68.8% and 73.3%, respectively. This indicates that the sensitivity of S1 was higher than S2 and S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of stoichiometry and binding constant of complex
ce:section.ce:para: The Job’s continuous variation method was employed to investigate the stoichiometry of chemosensor-TNP complex. In this method, the concentrations of chemosensor and TNP were varied, while the sum of the two concentrations was kept constant at 10 μM. A change in the measurable parameter that is proportional to complex formation (S1/S2/S3:TNP) was plotted against the mole fractions of TNP. The Job’s plot indicated 1:1 stoichiometry of the complexation between host and guest. Maximum change in fluorescence intensity was observed when mole fraction of TNP reached 0.5 as shown in Fig. 5 [67,71].

Analyses of the fluorescence data were also performed using Hill’s plot. A plot was obtained by following Eq. (3):
(3)
(
F
0
− F
)
/
(
F −
F
∞
)
=
[
P A
]
n
/
K
d i s

The binding capacity (n) was obtained by plotting log [(F 0 − F)/(F − F ∞)] against log[TNP], where F 0 − F and F ∞ are the relative fluorescence intensities of the chemosensor alone and the chemosensor saturated with the [TNP], respectively. The slope of the double-logarithm plot obtained from the experimental data represented binding capacity (n). The fluorescence response fitted to a Hill coefficient of 1 (1.193 (S1), 1.023 (S2), 1.258 (S3)) as shown in Fig. 6 . It is thus consistent with the formation of a 1:1 stoichiometry for the chemosensor: TNP complex [72,73].

To further confirm the stoichiometric ratio of the complex, Benesi–Hildebrand plot as given in Eq. (4) was analyzed. In addition to it, the Benesi–Hildebrand plot is also useful in calculating the association constant (Ka).
(4) 1/(F 0 − F i) = 1/{Ka * (F 0 − F min) * [TNP]} + 1/(F 0 − F min)
Here F0, Fi and Fmin implies fluorescence intensity of sensor, the fluorescence intensity obtained with TNP at different concentrations and the fluorescence intensity obtained with the addition of excess amount of TNP respectively [74,75]. From the plot of [1/(F0–Fi)] against [TNP]−1, the value of Ka is found to be 5.61 × 104 M−1 (S1), 5.2 × 104 M−1 (S2), 2.25 × 104 M−1 (S3). These high values of association constant (Ka) indicate that strong complex formation takes place between TNP and chemosensors (Fig. 7 ). The linearity of Benesi–Hildebrand plot further justifies that their exit 1:1 stoichiometric ratio between chemosensors and TNP [22].

The detection limit was calculated through linear fitting of the (Imax-I)/(Imax-Imin) vs log[TNP], where Imax, I and Imin are the initial fluorescence intensity, fluorescence intensity at a particular concentration of TNP and fluorescence intensity at saturation point respectively. The intercept at X-axis shows the lowest concentrations of TNP which can be detected by the respective chemosensors. Later by making use of the formula, ([TNP] × MWTNP)/1000 (multiplied by 109 to get the values in ppb), where MWTNP is the molecular weight of TNP, detection limit was calculated [44,76]. The detection limit for S1, S2, and S3 was found to be 738 ppb, 684 ppb and 686 ppb, respectively (Fig. ES16).

To investigate that whether the so designed chemosensors offered rapid response time to detect TNP, time dependant fluorescence titration were carried out. The changes in fluorescence intensity of S1, S2 and S3 on addition of TNP were studied. The fluorescence intensity of S1, S2 and S3 at 412.92 nm, 408.9 nm and 400.9 nm, respectively reached a maximum at an interval of 1 min as shown in Fig. 8 . The concentration of TNP was 50 μM. These results indicate that S1, S2 and S3 serve as highly potent chemosensors with short response time that is comparable or even superior to the methods reported earlier as shown in Table ES1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FONPs synthesis and fluorescence study of TNP in 90% aqueous DMSO medium
ce:section.ce:para: Due to greater solubility of TNP in water, there is a great threat of water contamination by TNP. Hence the development of efficient chemosensor that could detect TNP in an aqueous medium is very much required. In order to explore the efficiency of the chemosensors in aqueous media, the FONPs suspensions (VDMSO:Vwater −1:9) of S1, S2 and S3 were titrated with aqueous solution of TNP. The FONPs of TAI were successfully fabricated following re-precipitation approach. The TEM images of FONPs (Fig. 9 ) pictures the formation of highly dispersed spherical shaped FONPs with particle size of 2–3 nm. To obtain FONPs of such a small size was pretty impressive. The FONPs were present in aggregated state. The application of ultra sonication assisted in formation of these dispersed nanoaggregates. The aqueous suspension of these FONPs was stable for more than a month. The as synthesized FONPs of S1, S2 and S3 enabled detection of TNP in aqueous medium (VDMSO:Vwater −1:9).

The quenching efficiency of S1, S2, and S3 was found to be quietly impressive as FONPs (VDMSO:Vwater −1:9) as in non-aqueous medium as shown in Fig. 10 The fluorescence intensity of S1, S2, and S3 was gradually quenched up to the addition of 100 μM, 110 μM and 110 μM of TNP. The S-V plots of S1, S2 and S3 were found to be linear at low concentration of TNP whereas at its high concentration the S-V plots deviate from linearity (Fig. ES17). The association constant (Ka) found through linearity of Benesi–Hildebrand plot 1.17 × 104 M−1(S1), 2.6 × 104 M−1(S2) and 2.0 × 104 M−1(S3) was also impressive (Fig. ES18). The detection limits of the sensors for TNP were calculated to be 877.13 ppb, 824.76 ppb, and 894.43 ppb, respectively (Fig. ES19). The quenching constant was found to be 1.168 × 104 M−1 (S1), 2.65 × 104 M−1 (S2) and 2.05 × 104 M−1 (S3) (Fig. ES20). These ideal experimental results thus indicate the practical applicability of chemosensors towards TNP detection in multiple mediums.

Tap water, distilled water, and drinking water were spiked with TNP (100 μM) in order to investigate fluorescence sensing of TNP in real samples. The quenching efficiency of chemosensors towards TNP in real samples was investigated as shown in Fig. 11 . The impressive quenching efficiency of S1, S2 and S3 towards TNP in real water samples justified the applicability of chemosensors for field applications .[77]

The fluorescence spectrum of S1 was recorded in DMSO: H2O solution (1:9) by adjusting pH of the solutions using 0.1 N NaOH and 0.1 N HCl [78]. It was observed that the fluorescence emission of S1 was poor in an acidic environment which could be attributed to the formation of ammonium cationic salt. The sensor gave appreciably good fluorescence response in neutral and slightly basic and acidic pH, thus showing the effective functioning of chemosensor in wide pH range (Fig. 12 (a)). The quenching efficiency of S1 was prominently affected especially at very low pH and very high pH (Fig. 12(b)). The quenching efficiency of S1 was found to be strongly enhanced between pH 4–7 because as the pH of the medium reaches neutrality, the formation of electrostatic adduct between chemosensor and TNP was feasible in neutral and slightly basic and acidic pH. Whereas no such complexation was feasible at extremely high/low pH [79]. This result indicates that TNP detection by the chemosensor is tolerant to a normal range of solution pHs.

To investigate the selectivity of our chemosensors towards TNP, we carried out fluorescence titrations of chemosensors with various structural analogues to TNP like 2,4-Dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), 2-Nitrophenol (2-NP), 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP), 1,3-Dinitrobenzene (1,3-DNB), Nitrobenzene (NB), Phenol (Ph), 2-Nitrotoluene (2-NT), 4-Nitrotoluene (4-NT), Nitromethane (NM). The selectivity was evaluated in the presence of higher concentrations of these nitroaromatic compounds (Fig. 13 ). The fluorescence titrations were carried out by taking these analogues nitroaromatic compounds at a concentration of 200 μM. These compounds showed insignificant fluorescence quenching towards the chemosensors. This implies that even on the addition of these compounds at a concentration of approximately double than that of TNP, they do not exhibit any considerable quenching behavior and thus does not interfere in the estimation of TNP.

In order to investigate the binding sites of TNP, as well as to ascertain the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, we carried out 1H NMR studies of S1, S2, and S3 in the presence of TNP (in DMSO-d 6). There occurred prominent downfield shift of imidazole NH proton of S1, S2, and S3. This downfield shift of NH proton occurs possibly due to protonation of N-atom by TNP (Fig. ES21-ES23). In the case of S1, the OH group proton also showed a downfield shift from δ8.6313 to δ9.3916; indicating the participation of OH group in electrostatic interaction with TNP (Fig. ES21).

While analyzing the possible mechanism behind fluorescence quenching of chemosensor on addition of TNP, we firstly took a closer look at the absorption spectrum of TNP and emission spectrum of chemosensors. It revealed that there exists appreciable spectral overlap between these two. In such condition, there is a possibility of Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). Hence to find out whether it is a governing factor for fluorescence quenching, time-dependent fluorescence of chemosensors with and without TNP was measured. The fluorescence lifetime profiles of chemosensors don’t change on addition of TNP (Fig. 14 ). This means that the quenching occurred via static mechanism and not through FRET; if FRET occurred there would have been a change in fluorescence lifetime on the addition of TNP [47,64,80]. Thus, it became evident that Inner filter effect (IFE) might be prevailing and was the main reason behind quenching. There occurred prominent spectral overlap between excitation/emission spectrum of chemosensors and absorption spectrum of TNP (Fig. ES24).

Although there exists a spectral overlap between absorption spectrum of 2,4-Dinitrophenol and 4-Nitrophenol and excitation/emission spectrum of chemosensors (Fig. ES25), but the good selective quenching efficiency of chemosensors towards TNP indicated that their prevailed some other mechanism also that accounted for good selectivity and sensitivity of chemosensors towards TNP.

In order to elucidate the reason behind such a good selectivity of chemosensors towards TNP over other interfering analytes, we first centered our attention towards detailed analysis of electrostatic complex formation. Since the fluorophore contains the basic imidazole-N group, TNP could interact with them through acid-base interactions. The difference in pKa values of TNP (0.38), 2,4-DNP (4.89) and 4-NP (7.15) accounts for the quenching efficiency order: TNP > 2,4-DNP > 4-NP [64]. The electron-deficient nature of TNP favored rapid proton transfer from OH of TNP to NH of fluorophore thus enabling amplified electrostatic interaction.
Then we also investigated for a possibility of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from the chemosensor to quencher.

The as-synthesized chemosensors possessed п-bond conjugation as well as electron rich functionalities that enabled efficient complexation with electron deficient TNP. The main essential condition required for PET is close proximity between probe and quencher, for this, we investigated for the chances of formation of electrostatic adduct that would facilitate efficient PET [33]. A dilute solution of S1, S2, and S3 was titrated with TNP (100 μM), and its absorption spectra were recorded. There was a minor increase in the intensity of the absorption band of chemosensors along with the formation of a new low-intense band at a higher wavelength (Fig. ES26). The gradual increase of absorbance suggests the formation of ground state complexation between TNP and chemosensors [81,82]. The occurrence of the ground-state charge-transfer complex formation contributed to static fluorescence quenching [44,83]. Thus UV/Vis results clearly indicated that there are strong interactions between TNP and chemosensors. Moreover, in accordance with the above 1H NMR data discussion done above, it is clear that there exist electrostatic interactions between TNP and chemosensors.

In order to rationalize the mechanism of PET, we calculated HOMO-LUMO energy levels of the chemosensors (S1, S2, and S3) based on cyclic voltammetry study (Fig.ES27-28 and Table ES2-ES3). The calculated values of LUMO are −3.705 eV for S1, −3.745 eV for S2, −3.799 eV for S3. And the corresponding HOMO levels are −6.995 eV for S1, −6.995 eV for S2, −7.069 eV for S3. Thus the cyclic voltammetry study clearly revealed that there exists electron transfer from the high energy LUMO of photo-excited chemosensors to low energy LUMO of TNP (-3.89 eV) which results in high fluorescence quenching response toward TNP [84].

[85]. For other interfering nitro analytes, 2,4-DNP (-3.32 eV) [86], 2-NP (-2.22 eV) [86], 4-NP (-2.22 eV), 1,3- DNB (-3.13 eV) [13], NB (-2.42 eV) [13], Ph (0.04 eV) [86], 2-NT (-2.75 eV) [87], 4-NT (-2.32 eV) [13], NM (-1.90 eV) [87]; their LUMO level lies at high energy level as compared to LUMO of chemosensors (Fig. 15 ).

A better sensing response of S1 in comparison to S2 and S3 could be explained through moderate enhancement by electron-donating effect (OH and OCH3 groups), due to which quenching becomes better. The fluorophore became more electron rich which facilitated in better electron transfer (electrostatic interaction) from the fluorophore to the quencher (TNP).

Thus we overall conclude that the electrostatic interaction driven PET, as well as IFE, resulted in such a good sensing response of chemosensors towards TNP. The so synthesized chemosensors for TNP detection showed comparable or superior results to the methods reported earlier as shown in Table ES4.

For efficient on-site detection of explosive compounds, fluorescent test strips serve as best candidates for fast, reliable and visual on site trace detection of TNP [65]. The test strips were brightly fluorescent when seen under UV chamber. On addition of TNP dark spot was observed on fluorescent test strips of chemosensors (Fig. 16 ). The quenching behavior of chemosensors S1, S2, and S3 was applicable by varying the concentration of TNP up to a level of 10−8 M. The visual detection limits [88] of S1 solution coated paper a strip was thus calculated and was found to be 0.04 ng mm−2 when TNP concentration was 10−8 M. Such a good detection limit through solution coated paper strips of chemosensor could enable instant visual detection of TNP with high sensitivity.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized novel triaryl-substituted imidazoles and utilized them as TNP chemosensors. In recent years the use of explosive compounds for making IEDs has been increased for anti-human activities, therefore a low cost, highly sensitive and on site TNP detector has been reported. Our goal of designing chemosensors for rapid and on-site TNP detection was achieved through applicability of this sensor array in analyzing real water samples. Therefore, imidazole derivatives serve as a suitable class of compounds for the development of efficient sensors for the detection of TNP in solution or contact mode through a significant color change. The detection of TNP was attained at good selectivity without interference from other nitroaromatics like 1, 3-DNB, 2, 4-DNP, 2-NT, 4-NT, etc. The excellent signal response towards TNP was attributed to the synergistic effect of PET, IFE and electrostatic interaction between chemosensors and TNP. The high values of quenching constant/association constant indicates a good sensitivity of chemosensors towards TNP. The FONPs of chemosensors displayed good sensitivity towards TNP in aqueous medium also, thus enabling detection of TNP in multiple mediums. The prepared assay system comprising of FONPs was facile and low cost and thus can be effectively applied in environmental chemosensing applications. The present work can be extended to the synthesis of new fluorescent compounds as chemosensor for various other explosive compounds.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to IIT Ropar for NMR; SAIF, Panjab University, Chandigarh for ESI–MS, TEM; SIC, IIT Indore for CV; IIC, IIT Roorkee for FLS; DST-FIST New Delhi, for financial assistance for acquiring the FTIR, UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The author (J.) is thankful to MHRD and NIT Jalandhar for providing the research fellowship. We are thankful to Miss Priya Arora and Mr Harminder Singh for providing various suggestions in manuscript writing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.145.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jigyasa is a doctoral student in Department of Chemistry, Dr B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab, India. She is working in designing of chemosensors for detection of hazardous substances.

Dr Jaspreet Kaur Rajput is an Associate Professor in Department of Chemistry, Dr B.R. Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab, India. Her research interests are synthetic organic chemistry, nanocatalysis, advance materials and sensors.
